title: AgentDose: Steroid Dose Extraction from OMOP
subtitle: Three-method framework: structured fields, NLP parser, and LLM agent
problem: Corticosteroid dose is one of the most clinically important yet systematically missing variables in EHR-based research. In OMOP CDM, structured dose fields (amount_value, quantity) are missing in 62.7% and 97.9% of corticosteroid records respectively. The remaining dosing information is encoded in free-text prescription signature (sig) fields — short standardized strings like 'Take 1 tablet (5mg) by mouth twice daily' — but extracting structured, comparable dose estimates from these fields at scale has not been solved in an OMOP-compatible way.
approach: AgentDose implements three progressively richer extraction methods: (1) Baseline — uses only available structured OMOP fields (amount_value, quantity); (2) NLP Parser — combines structured fields with regex-based parsing of sig text to extract dose, frequency, route, and duration; (3) LLM Agent — uses Claude Opus 4 with structured prompting to handle all fields, including sigs that resist rule-based extraction. All methods output daily dose in prednisone-equivalent mg/day. Performance is evaluated against a manually adjudicated gold standard using MAE, MBE, and episode coverage.
impact: The LLM Agent achieved MAE of 5.2 mg/day and MBE of 2.15 mg/day across 478 of 687 usable episodes (69.6% coverage) — compared to the NLP Parser (MAE 12.6, MBE 7.6, 83.5% coverage) and Baseline (MAE 23.5, MBE 21.8, 75% coverage on only 3 of 4 episodes). Nominated for Best Community Contribution Showcase at OHDSI Global Symposium 2025. Being developed as an open-source R package for reproducible steroid exposure calculation across distributed OHDSI networks. Future work will extend to free-text clinical notes in EHR systems.
myRole: Led development of the full extraction framework — designed and implemented all three methods, built the evaluation pipeline, adjudicated gold-standard labels, and analyzed results. Presented at OHDSI Global Symposium 2025. Leading open-source R package development for multi-site deployment.

pipeline.label: Cohort Selection
pipeline.description: Dermatomyositis patients identified from OMOP CDM using validated ICD-10 phenotyping algorithms. All corticosteroid prescriptions in the drug_exposure table are extracted for the target cohort.

pipeline.label: Data Extraction
pipeline.description: For each prescription record: structured fields (drug concept, amount_value, quantity, days_supply) are pulled directly from OMOP; the sig free-text field ('Take 1 tablet (5mg) by mouth twice daily') is parsed separately by the NLP parser and LLM agent.

pipeline.label: Daily Dose Calculation
pipeline.description: Three methods compute daily dose in mg/day: (1) Baseline uses structured fields only; (2) NLP Parser applies regex patterns to sig text; (3) LLM Agent uses Claude Opus 4 with structured prompting. All outputs are converted to prednisone-equivalent mg/day.

pipeline.label: Model Evaluation
pipeline.description: Extracted doses compared to manually adjudicated gold standard. Metrics: Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Mean Bias Error (MBE), and episode coverage rate. LLM Agent: MAE 5.2, MBE 2.15, 478/687 episodes. NLP Parser: MAE 12.6, MBE 7.6, 96/115 episodes. Baseline: MAE 23.5, MBE 21.8, 3/4 episodes.

demoNote: Prednisone 10 MG Oral Tablet | Sig: Take 2 tablets (20mg total) by mouth every morning for 14 days, then take 1 tablet (10mg) daily thereafter. Dispense: 42 tablets. Refills: 0.

problems.text: Sig text detected — rule-based parser applied
spans: Take 2 tablets (20mg total) by mouth every morning for 14 days

problems.text: Taper instruction detected — requires multi-phase parsing

spans: Take 2 tablets (20mg total) by mouth every morning for 14 days

assessment.text: NLP Parser: extracted Phase 1 dose correctly; taper missed → MAE elevated
spans: Take 2 tablets (20mg total) by mouth every morning

assessment.text: LLM Agent: both phases extracted; prednisone-equivalent computed per phase

plan.text: Output: avg daily dose = 15.2 mg/day prednisone-equivalent (episode-weighted)
Dispense quantity (42 tablets) cross-validated against computed episode length (28 days × 1.5 avg tablets/day)

futureNote:
ASSESSMENT / PLAN — Rheumatology Follow-Up

Dermatomyositis: Continuing to improve. CK is down to 320 from 1,800 at diagnosis. She has been tolerating the current prednisone well. We will drop from 20mg to 15mg starting next week. If CK and aldolase remain stable at her four-week check, reduce to 10mg. Plan to taper by 5mg every four weeks thereafter, targeting 5mg daily as maintenance. Do not taper below 5mg without recheck.

Azathioprine: Remains at 150mg daily. No cytopenia on recent CBC. Continue current dose; consider reducing to 100mg if CK normalizes over the next two months.

Hydroxychloroquine: 400mg daily, unchanged.

GI prophylaxis: Omeprazole 20mg daily while on steroids. Discontinue when prednisone reaches 5mg daily.

Bone protection: Calcium 1000mg + Vitamin D 800IU daily. Repeat DEXA in 12 months.

Return in four weeks or sooner if new weakness, skin flare, or difficulty swallowing.

medications.text: Prednisone: 20mg → 15mg next week; taper to 10mg, then −5mg q4wks → 5mg maintenance

taper by 5mg every four weeks thereafter, targeting 5mg daily as maintenance

Azathioprine dose contingent on future labs — conditional extraction required

DEXA repeat in 12 months; Ca + Vit D for bone protection
Omeprazole to discontinue when prednisone reaches 5mg daily

title: Disease Severity Framework
subtitle: A multilevel, multidimensional conceptual framework for representing disease severity
problem: Disease severity is a central concept in clinical medicine — it drives treatment intensity, prognosis, escalation decisions, and resource allocation. Yet there is no unified conceptual or computational framework for representing it. Existing severity scores (APACHE-II, SOFA, Child-Pugh) are domain-specific predictive instruments that were designed to predict outcomes — mortality, ICU resource use, transplant priority — and have been reified over time into measures of disease state itself. That shift from predictive instrument to disease representation has consequences: we end up optimizing for the score, not the underlying state it was meant to capture.
approach: The framework I am developing has two axes. The first is multilevel: severity is not a single observation but a causal chain — from the genetic or molecular cause of disease, through cellular and systemic pathophysiological effects, through observable clinical events and their consequences, down to how the patient actually lives with the disease. The second axis is multidimensional: at every level, severity can be observed objectively (labs, vitals, imaging), subjectively (what the patient reports about pain, function, fatigue), and societally (how the disease disrupts work, relationships, and access to care). The thesis combines a PRISMA-ScR scoping review synthesizing how severity is defined across clinical domains with a formal conceptual framework that makes these axes explicit and computationally tractable.
impact: A manuscript is in preparation. The framework is intended to serve as conceptual groundwork for severity phenotyping research, risk stratification systems, and clinical decision support tools that go beyond single-number scores. The interactive diagram below illustrates the multilevel causal chain using sickle cell disease as a worked example — click any node to see how each level is characterized across the three dimensions.
myRole: Primary investigator: scoping review design, literature screening, conceptual framework development, manuscript writing. PI: Dr. Harold P. Lehmann, Johns Hopkins School of Medicine.

pipeline.label: Scoping Review
pipeline.description: PRISMA-ScR protocol across PubMed, EMBASE, and clinical informatics literature — synthesizing how severity is defined, operationalized, and measured across cardiology, oncology, neurology, hematology, and other domains. Coding for level (cause through experience) and dimension (objective, subjective, societal).

pipeline.label: Framework Design
pipeline.description: Developing the multilevel axis (@Cause → @Intermediate → @Journey → @Outcome → @PatientExperience) and the multidimensional axis (Objective / Subjective / Societal), synthesized from scoping review findings and tested against clinical vignettes including sickle cell disease, heart failure, and COPD.

pipeline.label: Formalization
pipeline.description: Translating the conceptual framework into a temporal state-transition model: severity as a moving location in a multilevel × multidimensional space, with transitions governed by disease progression, treatment response, and contextual factors.

pipeline.label: Validation
pipeline.description: Validating framework coverage against existing severity scores (SOFA, APACHE-II, Child-Pugh, NYHA, ECOG) and clinical vignettes, identifying which levels and dimensions each score captures — and which it systematically omits.

pipeline.label: Manuscript
pipeline.description: Manuscript in preparation for Journal of Biomedical Informatics. The framework will be released alongside an open-source evidence synthesis platform developed in parallel with the thesis.

decisionNodes.label: Molecular Cause
decisionNodes.rationale: Severity begins at the causal root: the genetic variant, pathogen, or exposure that initiates disease. In sickle cell disease (SCD), a single point mutation — Glu→Val in the β-globin gene — produces HbS. Genotype (HbSS vs. HbSC vs. HbSβ⁺-thalassemia) and modifiers like HbF percentage and alpha-thalassemia co-inheritance determine the baseline severity ceiling. Two patients with 'sickle cell disease' can have vastly different severity trajectories based on these molecular differences alone.
decisionNodes.next: The molecular cause propagates severity through two parallel pathophysiological pathways: one primarily cellular (sickling, hemolysis), one primarily systemic (endothelial damage, coagulation, inflammation).

decisionNodes.label: Cellular Effects
decisionNodes.rationale: At the subcellular and cellular level, HbS polymerizes under hypoxia, deforming red cells into the sickle shape. This drives hemolysis: red cells lyse, releasing hemoglobin into plasma where it scavenges nitric oxide and generates reactive oxygen species. Objective markers of hemolysis — LDH, indirect bilirubin, reticulocyte count — capture severity at this level. These are measurable, but they don't tell you whether the patient is having a crisis today.
decisionNodes.next: Cellular hemolysis is one input into systemic disease. The other pathway — vaso-occlusion and endothelial injury — operates in parallel and drives a different set of clinical consequences.

decisionNodes.label: Systemic Effects
decisionNodes.rationale: Sickled cells are rigid and adhesive. They obstruct microvasculature, trigger endothelial activation, coagulation, and inflammation — driving the systemic pathophysiology that underlies most SCD complications. Tricuspid regurgitation velocity (TRV) measures pulmonary hypertension from chronic hemolysis. WBC count reflects inflammatory activation. This level is also where objective severity most clearly begins to diverge from patient experience: lab values can look stable while the patient is in significant pain.
decisionNodes.next: Both cellular and systemic pathophysiology converge into observable disease events — the crises, hospitalizations, and end-organ damage that mark the clinical trajectory.

decisionNodes.label: Events & Outcomes
decisionNodes.rationale: Disease events are where severity becomes clinically legible. In SCD: vaso-occlusive crises (VOC), acute chest syndrome (ACS), stroke, splenic sequestration, avascular necrosis, nephropathy, retinopathy. Severity at this level has both an objective face (GFR, 6-minute walk test, echocardiography) and a trajectory face: a patient with two ACS episodes this year is at a different point in their severity arc than one who had their first episode five years ago. VOC frequency and prior ACS are among the strongest predictors of future severity.
decisionNodes.next: Events and outcomes are the level most captured by existing severity scores — but they miss the molecular cause, the pathophysiology, and crucially, the patient experience.

decisionNodes.label: Patient Experience
decisionNodes.rationale: The final level — and the one most systematically excluded from clinical severity scoring — is how patients actually live with the disease. In SCD, this means chronic pain that is rarely fully captured by hospitalization counts (most crises are managed at home), fatigue, opioid dependency risk, school and work absence, and the psychosocial burden of a stigmatized, undertreated disease. The ASCQ-Me (Adult Sickle Cell Quality of Life Measurement) captures SCD-specific PROs. The societal dimension adds SDoH: access to care, housing stability, insurance. A severity score that ignores this level is measuring the disease, not the patient.
decisionNodes.next: A complete severity representation integrates all five levels — cause, cellular effects, systemic effects, clinical events, and patient experience — across objective, subjective, and societal dimensions. No existing single score does this.

title: Infectious Safety of Immunosuppressants in Rheumatic Disease
subtitle: Comparative risk of serious infection across immunosuppressive regimens using OMOP CDM
status: Active · Myositis Center, Johns Hopkins Rheumatology
problem: Patients with rare rheumatic diseases — dermatomyositis, systemic lupus, systemic sclerosis, uveitis — require long-term immunosuppression, but treatment carries meaningful risk of serious and opportunistic infections including PML, PJP, and VZV. Because these diseases are rare, single-site studies are severely underpowered to characterize comparative infectious risk across agents. Current prophylaxis practices are driven largely by expert opinion rather than real-world comparative evidence.
approach: Using the OHDSI federated network across 6+ academic medical centers, I contribute to a distributed pharmacoepidemiologic study applying new-user, active-comparator design. Aim 1 evaluates monotherapy comparisons (mycophenolate, rituximab, IVIG, JAK inhibitors); Aim 2 evaluates combination immunosuppression regimens. Propensity score-adjusted Cox proportional hazards models and self-controlled case series (SCCS) provide complementary causal estimates. Analysis packages are deployed using HADES/Strategus so only aggregate results leave each site.
impact: Produces the first multi-site comparative estimates of serious infection risk — including rare opportunistic infections — across major immunosuppressive regimens used in rheumatic diseases. Results across 6+ sites will directly inform prophylaxis guidelines and shared decision-making for clinicians managing these patients.
myRole: Contributed to disease cohort phenotyping, study design, and comparative effectiveness analysis framework. PI: Dr. Christopher A. Mecoli, Johns Hopkins Rheumatology.

pipeline.label: Disease Phenotyping
pipeline.description: Computable phenotypes defined for target rheumatic diseases (DM, SLE, SSc, uveitis) using ICD-10 concept sets, validated with CohortDiagnostics across all participating sites before analysis begins.

pipeline.label: Drug Exposure Cohorts
pipeline.description: New-user cohorts defined at first prescription of each comparator drug (MMF, RTX, IVIG, JAKi). Active-comparator design ensures exchangeability assumptions hold at cohort entry.

pipeline.label: PS Derivation
pipeline.description: Large-scale propensity scores estimated from hundreds of baseline covariates using regularized logistic regression (LASSO). Preference score distributions assessed for equipoise across sites.

pipeline.label: Outcome Ascertainment
pipeline.description: Primary outcomes: hospitalized infection, PJP, PML, VZV/Shingles. Each ascertained via validated OMOP concept sets. Both Cox PH and SCCS designs applied to test consistency of estimates.

pipeline.label: Distributed Analysis
pipeline.description: Strategus coordinates execution: analysis packages run locally at each site, sharing only aggregate hazard ratio estimates. Meta-analysis combines site-level results into network-wide comparative safety estimates.

title: Claims–EHR Diagnostic Synergy
subtitle: Completeness of diagnosis coding across 17 million patients and 56 healthcare organizations, 2010–2022
problem: Electronic health records have achieved near-universal adoption in U.S. hospitals, yet their completeness as a source of diagnostic information remains poorly characterized at scale. Clinicians, health systems, and value-based payers increasingly rely on EHR-derived diagnoses for risk stratification, comorbidity measurement, and quality reporting — but if EHRs systematically miss diagnoses that appear in administrative claims, those analyses are quietly biased. Prior comparisons were limited to single health systems or narrow disease areas; no large, multi-institutional, longitudinal characterization of the EHR-claims diagnostic gap existed.
approach: Using the Optum Labs Data Warehouse (OLDW), we linked de-identified claims and EHR data for ~17 million patient-years across 56 healthcare organizations (2010–2022). For each calendar year and each AHRQ Clinical Classification Software (CCS) diagnostic group, we computed the proportions of patient-years with diagnoses recorded exclusively in claims, exclusively in EHRs, or in both. Agreement metrics (sensitivity, specificity, Cohen's κ, PABAK) were calculated treating claims as the reference standard. Comorbidity burden (chronic condition counts and Charlson Comorbidity Index) was derived separately from each source. Linear regression modeled annual trends in the EHR-claims diagnostic gap across the full study period and the pre-COVID years (2010–2019).
impact: Across all years, ~45% of patient-diagnoses appeared only in claims and were absent from EHRs. EHR completeness improved ~10% from 2010 to 2019 (claims-only share declined from 51% to 41%), but reversed sharply after 2020. Claims-derived comorbidity was consistently higher: mean chronic condition count 4.42 vs. 2.04 in EHRs; mean CCI 0.74 vs. 0.39. The EHR-claims gap in CCI narrowed significantly pre-2020 but did not improve after the pandemic onset. These findings directly inform how health systems, payers, and researchers should weight EHR versus claims data for risk adjustment and population health analytics.
myRole: First and corresponding author. Designed and executed the full analysis: sample identification, CCS-based diagnosis linkage, agreement metric computation, comorbidity derivation, trend modeling, and manuscript writing. Co-authors: Harlan Pittell, PhD; Christopher Kitchen, MS; Elyse Lasser, DrPH; Hadi Kharrazi, MD, PhD (PI). Division of Biomedical Informatics and Data Science, Johns Hopkins.

pipeline.label: Sample Identification
pipeline.description: Enrollees with both claims and EHR data identified from OLDW (2010–2022). Patients with coverage gaps, conflicting demographics, or boundary-age Medicare overlap excluded. Final cohort: 16,991,756 patient-years across 56 healthcare organizations.

pipeline.label: Diagnosis Grouping
pipeline.description: All structured diagnosis codes (ICD-9 and ICD-10) from both sources collapsed into AHRQ CCS groups. Provisional, rule-out, unconfirmed, and NLP-derived diagnoses excluded to ensure comparability. Multiple same-condition occurrences within a year-source pair collapsed to one.

pipeline.label: Source Classification
pipeline.description: For each patient-year and CCS category, diagnoses classified as: claims-only, EHR-only, or both. Annual proportions computed and visualized as stacked bars stratified by CCS group and by healthcare organization.

pipeline.label: Agreement Metrics
pipeline.description: Claims treated as reference standard; EHR as index test. Sensitivity, specificity, Cohen's κ, and PABAK computed for each of 18 high-level and 88 mid-level CCS categories. Moderate-to-substantial agreement overall (κ = 0.60, PABAK = 0.65), with wide variation by category.

pipeline.label: Comorbidity & Trends
pipeline.description: Charlson Comorbidity Index and chronic condition counts derived separately from claims and EHR. Linear regression (Δt = β0 + β1(t − 2010)) modeled annual changes in the diagnostic gap. Pre-COVID (2010–2019) and full-period (2010–2022) trends estimated separately to isolate pandemic disruption.

title: LLM Agents for Research Reproducibility
subtitle: Multi-agent GPT-4o system autonomously replicates Alzheimer's Disease findings from NACC data · 5 published studies, 35 assertions
problem: Science is in a reproducibility crisis — an estimated 75–90% of published studies cannot be independently replicated. Within biomedical informatics, reproducing a prior study requires reading the manuscript, acquiring the dataset, standing up a compute environment, and often writing code from scratch. This is so time- and resource-intensive that large-scale audit of the scientific literature is effectively impossible. We asked: can LLM-based autonomous agents automate this process?
approach: We used the NACC Quick Access Uniform Dataset (UDS v3), a public longitudinal dataset covering 40,000+ Alzheimer's Disease patients across 35 ADRCs. From 328 papers citing the NACC v3 schema update, we selected 5 highly-cited, reproducible studies. We built a five-agent team — Planner, Engineer, Scientist, Critic, and Executor — using Microsoft Autogen v2 and GPT-4o. Each agent received only the abstract and methods section of the original paper plus curated NACC variable definitions. The team autonomously generated and executed Python code to reproduce abstract-reported findings, with minimal human-in-the-loop prompting at key decision points.
impact: Agents reproduced approximately 53% of key findings per study on average (25/35 assertions, 71.4%, across all studies). Boolean directional findings (racial disparities, symptom associations) were reliably reproduced; numeric estimates requiring domain-specific statistical methods (demographically-corrected regression, network bootstrapping) frequently diverged. Code execution failed in 47% of attempts but self-corrected in all cases. The work demonstrates both the promise of LLM agents as scalable reproducibility auditors and the current bottlenecks: missing method detail, domain-specific statistics, and complex cohort logic. All code and agent transcripts are publicly available.
myRole: Co-first author with equal contribution (Nicolas Dobbins, PhD). Led study selection and eligibility criteria design, conducted evaluation of agent-generated outputs across all 5 studies, drove statistical and process replication assessment, and co-wrote the manuscript. Co-authors: Kristine Lan, MS; Meliha Yetisgen, PhD. Affiliated with Biomedical Informatics & Data Science, Johns Hopkins University.

pipeline.label: Study Selection
pipeline.description: 328 papers citing the NACC UDS v3 update screened. Filtered to 101 with ≥10 citations, then to 5 clinical studies fully reproducible using only the Quick Access dataset. Criteria: no additional modules, no external datasets, full text accessible.

pipeline.label: Prompt Construction
pipeline.description: For each study: abstract and methods section extracted as plain text. 15–30 NACC data dictionary variables judged relevant to the study's analytic pipeline were manually curated. Dataset-level instructions (date handling, missing values, cohort inclusion/exclusion logic) appended. No additional information provided.

pipeline.label: Multi-Agent Execution
pipeline.description: Five-agent team (Planner, Engineer, Scientist, Critic, Executor) orchestrated by an Autogen manager. The Engineer wrote iterative Python code; the Executor ran it; the Critic and Scientist reviewed outputs. Code execution failed in 26/55 attempts (47%) and self-corrected in all cases. Mean turn count: 35.6 per study.

pipeline.label: Assertion Evaluation
pipeline.description: Key findings manually extracted from each abstract as individual assertions (numeric or boolean). Agent outputs evaluated for alignment: numeric values within ±1 unit considered reproduced; boolean assertions matched if direction agreed. Evaluated across three dimensions: assertion-level accuracy, statistical method consistency, and workflow process fidelity.

pipeline.label: Error Analysis
pipeline.description: Systematic review of mismatches revealed recurring failure modes: omission of demographic covariates in regression, wrong reference group choice, domain-specific methods (network bootstrapping, SRB z-scores) skipped or approximated, and complex temporal cohort logic not implemented. Studies with explicit, simple analytic descriptions converged faster and reproduced more accurately.

title: Racial Disparities in Post-Hysterectomy ED Utilization
subtitle: State-based retrospective cohort study using HCUP data · Florida & Maryland 2017–2021
status: In Press · Int J Gynecol Obstet, 2026
problem: Racial disparities in post-surgical outcomes are well-documented, but less is known about unplanned healthcare utilization — particularly emergency department visits within 30 days of discharge. Hysterectomy is one of the most common surgical procedures in the US (400,000+ per year), with Black women undergoing it at 3.5× the rate of White women. Understanding what drives post-operative ED utilization, and whether disparities persist after adjusting for clinical and socioeconomic factors, is essential to targeting quality improvement interventions.
approach: Retrospective cohort study using HCUP State Inpatient (SID), State Ambulatory (SASD), and State Emergency Department (SEDD) databases for Florida and Maryland, 2017–2021 — covering 48M+ encounters. I identified 193,432 hysterectomy cases via ICD-10-PCS and CPT procedure codes, then linked to subsequent ED encounters within 30 days. Multivariable logistic regression estimated adjusted ORs for 30-day ED visits, with race/ethnicity as the primary exposure. Interaction terms assessed effect modification by state, surgery route, and setting. A race × state interaction model examined geographic heterogeneity. Secondary outcome: total hospital charges modeled via generalized linear model (Gamma distribution). STROBE reporting guidelines followed.
impact: Accepted and in press at International Journal of Gynecology & Obstetrics (2026). Among 193,432 hysterectomy patients, 11.3% had 30-day ED visits. Black patients had significantly higher odds (OR 1.16, 95% CI 1.12–1.20) after full adjustment; the disparity was attenuated in Maryland vs. Florida (interaction OR 0.90, p=0.011). Medicaid patients had more than twice the odds of private insurance (OR 2.09). Maryland's all-payer TCOC model was associated with lower costs and ED utilization. Findings highlight modifiable structural factors — access to follow-up care, insurance type, state-level policy — as targets for equity interventions.
myRole: First and corresponding author. Designed the cohort selection and linkage pipeline, conducted all statistical analyses in R, generated all results, drafted and revised the manuscript. Co-authors: Yiheng Wang, Chen Dun, Mostafa A. Borahay, Hadi Kharrazi (PI). Division of Biomedical Informatics and Data Science, Johns Hopkins.

pipeline.label: HCUP Databases
pipeline.description: Florida and Maryland HCUP SID, SASD, and SEDD databases (2017–2021) — 48M+ inpatient, outpatient, and ED encounters. Patient-level procedure, diagnosis, demographic, and payer data standardized across states.

pipeline.label: Cohort Selection
pipeline.description: Hysterectomy cases identified by ICD-10-PCS (inpatient) and CPT codes (outpatient). Radical hysterectomy excluded. Data quality filters applied for valid patient identifiers and complete follow-up windows. Final cohort: 193,432 patients.

pipeline.label: Outcome Ascertainment
pipeline.description: Primary outcome: 30-day ED visit due to post-hysterectomy complications. ED encounters linked to index hospitalization within the same state database. Secondary outcome: total hospital charges, modeled with GLM Gamma distribution.

pipeline.label: Regression Modeling
pipeline.description: Multivariable logistic regression with race/ethnicity as primary exposure, adjusting for age, CCI, surgery setting/route/indication, primary payer, hospital state, and median household income quartile. Interaction terms: race × state, race × surgery setting, and surgery route × setting.

pipeline.label: Disparity Analysis
pipeline.description: Adjusted ORs reported with 95% CIs. Race × state interaction model (Table 3) estimated geographic heterogeneity in racial disparities. Sensitivity analysis substituted Area Deprivation Index for household income quartile — results were consistent.
